lesson.name: Conflict Resolution and Strategy - Lesson 3 — Dynamics, Mediation, Mobilization

messages.content:
### Summary: Dynamics, Mediation, and Mobilization in Memory Studies

This section of *Dynamics, Mediation, Mobilization* explores how memory is not a static archival record, but a fluid, mediated process that shapes social and political realities. Based on the scholarly framework of Ann Rigney, the key points are summarized below:

#### 1. The Dynamic Nature of Memory
*   **Memory as Process:** Collective memory is a "dynamic turn" (p. 18). It is constantly evolving through continuous acts of representation and re-interpretation.
*   **Stickiness and Differential Memorability:** Certain cultural narratives endure ("stick") more than others due to their aesthetic, emotional, or social resonance. Scholarly analysis must focus on why some narratives gain long-term traction while others fade (p. 17).
*   **Sedimentation and Timefulness:** Memory "secretes meaning" over time. Understanding these layers—the "memory-geology nexus"—helps bridge the gap between fixed past events and our fluid present (p. 18).

#### 2. Mediation and Agency
*   **The Agency of the Aesthetic:** Culture—through literature, film, art, and slogans—is an active agent. These forms do not just reflect the past; they create the infrastructure for future memory and can mobilize populations (p. 14).
*   **The Memory-Activism Nexus:** Memory is a tool for social movements. It serves as a mechanism for both resistance and solidarity. Scholars look at how grassroots actors turn "ordinary" events into "historical" ones to demand change (p. 16, 17).
*   **Mediation and Remediation:** Memory travels across various platforms (news, digital archives, performance). Each shift in medium creates new dynamics of remembering and forgetting (e.g., "media amnesia" during financial crises) (p. 19).

#### 3. Implications for Conflict and Resolution
*   **Divided Narratives:** Memory wars often stem from competing "scripts." Overcoming destructive or toxic conflicts requires identifying these scripts and developing more creative and productive alternative narratives to foster solidarity (p. 17).
*   **Inclusivity and Justice:** Memory work (such as apologetic acts or the re-signification of traumatic sites like the ESMA in Argentina) is essential for justice. True reconciliation requires moving beyond performative forgetting toward substantive structural change (p. 18, 20).

**Core Lesson for Application:**
To apply these concepts to management or mediation, one must view disputes not as fixed points of history, but as **dynamic, mediated processes**. Conflict resolution involves analyzing the "scripts" that fuel the dispute, understanding the "materiality" of the grievances (the physical objects or symbols at stake), and facilitating a transition from rigid narratives to flexible, future-oriented memory practices.

Lesson 3 — Dynamics, Mediation, Mobilization - Presentation4 slides
Dynamics, Mediation, and Mobilization
  • Memory as a dynamic, evolving cultural process (p. 14).
  • Mediation and remediation shape how we remember (p. 14).
  • Mobile memory links cultural forms to social mobilization (p. 14).

This document serves as the front matter, publication details, and acknowledgments for the scholarly work *Dynamics, Mediation, Mobilization: Doing Memory Studies With Ann Rigney*, published as Volume 41 of the *Media and Cultural Memory* series.

Key points include:

Academic Scope and Editorship
The volume is edited by Astrid Erll, Susanne Knittel, and Jenny Wüstenberg (p. 4). It features contributions from a distinguished editorial board comprising prominent scholars in the field of memory studies (p. 3). The work is presented as an encyclopedia-style collection dedicated to the research of Ann Rigney (p. 6).

Publication and Licensing
The book was published in 2024 by Walter de Gruyter GmbH, Berlin/Boston. It is an open-access publication funded by the Goethe University Frankfurt am Main Open Access Publication Fund (p. 5). The text is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, allowing for open reuse, provided that third-party material is cleared by the user (p. 5).

Production and Acknowledgments
The editors acknowledge the collaborative effort of over fifty authors who provided chapters under a short timeline. Furthermore, they express gratitude to the staff at De Gruyter, research assistants including Bilyana Manolova, and the supporting institutions that enabled the project's completion (p. 6).

This text serves as the introduction to a *liber amicorum* dedicated to Ann Rigney, a foundational figure in memory studies. The volume outlines the core theoretical and methodological contributions Rigney has made to the field while setting an agenda for future research.

Key themes and concepts from the text include:

Understanding Memory Dynamics
Rigney defines memory not as a static occurrence, but as an ongoing process of cultural representation and circulation. Central to this approach is the concept of mediation and remediation, which describes how memory travels across different media—such as literature, film, and digital platforms—to shape historical experience.

The Agency of the Aesthetic
A defining feature of Rigney’s scholarship is the "agency of the aesthetic." This suggests that cultural forms do more than reflect history; they actively shape how events are remembered and can serve as powerful tools for social mobilization. Events are viewed as having a "slow-release" impact, expressed through stories that are told and retold over time.

Methodological Approaches
Researching memory "with Ann Rigney" involves a multidisciplinary, ecological approach. Instead of analyzing isolated objects, it treats various items—from novels and street signs to archival materials—as a networked "archive of memory." This method demands close reading, comparative analysis, and longitudinal perspectives to understand how materials gain or lose meaning across different national and cultural contexts.

Memory Studies as a Community
Beyond her theoretical innovation, the text highlights Rigney’s role as a mentor and builder of the field. As a "founding mother" of the Memory Studies Association, she prioritized building an inclusive, global academic community. Her mentorship style is described as formative, encouraging researchers to develop their own voices while fostering interdisciplinarity.

Structure of the Book
The volume is organized into eight major research areas that Rigney helped shape (pp. 12-13, 16):
1. History, Narrative, and Memory: Investigating how the past is constructed through linear discourse.
2. The Dynamics of Memory: Exploring the fluidity and movement of collective recollection.
3. Mediation and Remediation: Analyzing the role of media technologies and platforms.
4. Life and Afterlives of Memory: Tracing the longevity and evolution of cultural narratives.
5. National and Transnational Memory: Examining memory beyond borders and toward global justice.
6. The Matter of Memory: Addressing the materiality of objects and their role in cultural remembrance.
7. The Agency of the Aesthetic: Studying the power of art to counter "planetary amnesia."
8. The Memory-Activism Nexus: Investigating how memories operate within social movements and political change.

Overall, the text positions this volume as both a celebration of past achievements and an encyclopedia for future memory research (p. 15).

This document serves as an introduction to the interdisciplinary field of memory studies, centered on the scholarship of Ann Rigney. It categorizes the field into six thematic sections, outlined as follows:

1. Narrativity and History (pp. 17): This section explores the nexus of memory and narrative. Scholars examine how the past is constructed through storytelling, the role of "prememory" in future acts of remembrance, and the distinction between "story" and "script" in resolving transnational memory conflicts.

2. The Dynamics of Memory (pp. 17-18): Moving away from the idea of memory as a static repository, this section conceptualizes it as a dynamic process in flux. Key concepts like the "stickiness of stories," "differential memorability," and the "memory-geology nexus" highlight how collective memory is constantly negotiated through environmental and social contexts.

3. Mediation and Remediation (pp. 19): This part focuses on the media technologies that store and circulate cultural memory. It examines the "platformization" of memory, the integration of artificial intelligence into remembrance culture, and how media can facilitate both the production and the "amnesia" (forgetting) of historical events.

4. Life and Afterlives of Memory (pp. 19-20): This section adopts a longitudinal perspective, focusing on the trans-temporal journey of texts and cultural phenomena. It discusses "afterlives" in terms of literary history, protest slogans, and musical memory, emphasizing that later communities use these afterlives to sustain their own identities.

5. National and Transnational Memory (pp. 21-22): These chapters challenge "methodological nationalism" by mapping how memories circulate across borders. While the nation-state remains a significant arena for memory work, scholars here analyze how decolonial, digital, and activist networks articulate identities beyond state boundaries.

6. The Matter of Memory (pp. 22-23): This final section explores the materiality of memory, moving beyond discourse to consider objects, bodies, and landscapes as co-agents of memory. It introduces an ecological approach that studies the continuous interaction between humans and non-human entities, including "unwanted heritage" and "necrotoxicity" in post-conflict sites.

The section covering pages 61 to 112 focuses on the "Dynamics of Memory" and "Mediation and Remediation," exploring how memories are processed, stored, and adapted over time.

Dynamics of Memory
This subsection addresses the shifting nature of collective memory. Key considerations include:
- The Dynamic Turn: Jeffrey K. Olick introduces the concept of memory as a process in constant motion rather than a static phenomenon. 
- Postmemory: Marianne Hirsch analyzes the intergenerational transmission of traumatic memory, noting how descendants inherit and reshape the experiences of the past.
- Political and Collective Contexts: Ido de Haan examines the political economy of memory, while Jenny Wüstenberg discusses the uncovering of "mnemonic fossils." William Hirst emphasizes the role of flashbulb memories in shaping collective remembrance.
- Agency and Representation: Researchers investigate how specific groups, such as women remembering chattel slavery, and specific sites, like those in Argentina, undergo "transmutations" of meaning. Experimental work, such as Susannah Radstone’s study on "Citrus," further illustrates the diverse methods used to observe how memory is constructed.

Mediation and Remediation
This subsection analyzes how media technologies and platforms influence the longevity and accessibility of cultural memory:
- Prosthetic Memory: Alison Landsberg discusses how mediation allows individuals to inhabit memories of events they did not personally experience.
- Platformization: José van Dijck and Rik Smit explore how digital platforms dictate the "scholarly legacy" and the infrastructure of how we remember in the digital age.
- Technological Impact: The section bridges traditional media with modern challenges, including the role of Artificial Intelligence in memory, the use of cultural analytics in research, and the "media amnesia" associated with capital accumulation.

The overarching theme of these sections is that memory is not a fixed historical record but an active, mediated practice that evolves through technological, political, and cultural shifts.

This section explores the intricate relationship between technology, media, and the formation of collective memory, focusing on how contemporary digital platforms and analytical tools shape our engagement with the past.

Mediation and Prosthetic Memory
Alison Landsberg introduces the concept of mediation, emphasizing how modern technologies and cultural artifacts function as prosthetics for memory. These tools enable individuals to access experiences and histories they did not personally live, effectively expanding the boundaries of personal and collective remembrance through technological intervention.

The Platformization of Memory
José van Dijck and Rik Smit discuss the shift toward the platformization of scholarly legacy and memory. They analyze how digital platforms have moved from being neutral spaces to active participants in curation and archival practices. This infrastructural change alters how academic and cultural heritage is accessed, stored, and disseminated, creating new dynamics for how digital memory is managed.

Artificial Intelligence and Analytics
Anna Poletti examines the intersection of memory and artificial intelligence, highlighting how generative and algorithmic systems influence the construction of remembrance. Complementing this, Gerardine Meaney explores cultural analytics, demonstrating how large-scale data and computational methods can reveal patterns in cultural history that were previously obscured. These methods provide new empirical ways to study how stories circulate across immense datasets.

Media Amnesia and Capital
Laura Basu addresses the darker side of this landscape, identifying a form of media amnesia driven by capital accumulation. She argues that the economic imperatives underlying modern media platforms often incentivize constant "newness," which can lead to the systematic erasure or devaluation of deeper, historical narratives. This mechanism suggests that the structures of commercial media can actively work against the sustenance of cultural memory.

This section of the publication, spanning pages 149 to 200, focuses on the "Afterlives of Memory," "National and Transnational Memory," and "The Matter of Memory." The following points summarize the key thematic contributions within these chapters:

Afterlives of Memory
The contributors examine how cultural memory persists and evolves over time. Discussions include the "mnemohistory" of literature, the transformative potential of national apologies toward self-determination, and the utilization of protest slogans in cultural memory. Furthermore, the chapters explore how musical creativity, posthumanist frameworks, and even archival footnotes act as conduits for preserving and reshaping the past in modern contexts.

National and Transnational Memory
This segment investigates the interplay between local, national, and global memory frameworks. Central themes include the potential of transnational memory to inform justice, the collective global memory of crises like COVID-19, and the role of aesthetic politics in shaping national identity. Authors also highlight how memory translation and "minor transnationalism" challenge traditional, top-down narratives by focusing on specific, marginalized, or activist-driven perspectives.

The Matter of Memory
These chapters emphasize the materiality of cultural remembrance. The authors analyze how physical objects—ranging from "post-German" artifacts in Poland to bones of contention in the Spanish Civil War—possess mnemonic agency. By exploring the "accidental archives" and the intersection of ecology with cartographic collage, the text illustrates that memory is not merely a cognitive or narrative process but is deeply embedded in the physical, discarded, and material remnants of history.

The provided text covering pages 201–244 focuses on the multifaceted nature of memory, specifically its national, transnational, and material dimensions.

Transnational and Social Dynamics 
Chiara De Cesari re-evaluates the concept of transnational memory, emphasizing how memories cross borders and interact with global contexts. Nicole L. Immler explores the crucial connection between post-memory and justice frameworks, while Vered Vinitzky-Seroussi examines how artifacts such as flags and stamps served as vehicles for the transnational memory of the COVID-19 pandemic. Stijn Vervaet investigates the transnational memory work inherent in the art of Sanja Iveković regarding female partisans. Additionally, Margaret Kelleher links aesthetic expression to political history through the lens of Yeats’s Nobel Prize, and Eneken Laanes discusses the mechanisms of minor transnationalism and memory translation.

The Materiality of Memory
Liedeke Plate introduces the relationship between material objects and memory, highlighting the "memory of materials" themselves. Maria Zirra analyzes resistant periodical memory objects through the work of Gladys Mgudlandlu. Barbara Törnquist-Plewa discusses the agency of "post-German" artifacts within the Polish cultural landscape. Birgit Meyer looks at how provocative objects facilitate the remaking of cultural memory. Francisco Ferrándiz examines "necrotoxicity" in the context of the Francoist underworld, exploring the impact of physical remains. Lucy Bond and Jessica Rapson utilize cartographic collage to address "ecologies of the discarded," while Richard Crownshaw reflects on the ethics of who and what is prioritized in literary memory.

The section "The Matter of Memory" (pp. 245–296) examines the intersection between materiality and collective remembrance, focusing on how objects, archives, and physical environments function as active agents in shaping cultural memory.

Key thematic pillars include:

Materiality and Agency: The contributions emphasize that memory is not merely a cognitive or representational process but is deeply rooted in the "matter" of the world. Objects are shown to possess "mnemonic agency," meaning they actively influence how the past is retrieved, ignored, or re-signified across different cultural contexts.

Archival and Periodical Resistance: Scholars explore how "accidental archives"—often neglected or marginalized materials—can act as sites of resistance. By examining objects like historical periodicals or period-specific artifacts, the text demonstrates how "resistant memory objects" challenge dominant historical narratives and provide voices for those sidelined in mainstream history.

Transcultural and Contested Objects: Several chapters investigate the "transcultural mnemonic agency" of objects that travel between borders, such as "post-German" items found in Poland. These studies reveal how objects can be repurposed in new political landscapes, moving from their original cultural context to serve as triggers for complex, often fraught, communal identities.

Necrotoxicity and Ecological Memory: The text addresses the darker side of material memory, specifically "necrotoxicity" in the context of mass graves and historical violence. It highlights how the physical remains of the past (like bones or discarded items) continue to impose themselves on the present, requiring a form of "mnemonic recovery" that acknowledges the environment's role as a silent witness to historical trauma.

Relational Mnemonic Reading: A recurring methodology presented is "diffractive reading," which suggests that we should not look at memory objects in isolation but rather through their relationality—how they interact with other materials, spaces, and narratives to construct a multi-layered understanding of history. By analyzing the "ecologies of the discarded," the authors argue that understanding what we choose to keep or throw away is essential to mapping the human relationship with temporal continuity.

The section spanning pages 297 to 348 explores the relationship between cultural memory and various modes of aesthetic production, focusing on how art, literature, and performance act as agents in shaping the past.

The Agency of the Aesthetic

Michael Rothberg opens this section by discussing the "multidirectional" potential of memory, arguing that aesthetic forms do not merely reflect memory but actively intervene in how we perceive the relationship between diverse historical traumas (p. 297). This theme of aesthetic agency is further developed through specific case studies:

Family Chronicles and Performance
Susanne Knittel focuses on the scalability of memory, noting that family narratives utilize aesthetic strategies to bridge the gap between intimate history and broader cultural resonance (p. 305). Emilie Pine complements this by examining how theatrical performance mediates memory, suggesting that "An Old Song Half Forgotten" acts as a conduit for affiliation, where historical events are reified through the physical presence and labor of the performers (p. 333).

Environmental and Repair-Oriented Memory
Several contributors address how art addresses global crises. Stef Craps explores the concept of "planetary amnesia," arguing that creative arts play a vital role in preventing the ecological memory loss associated with the Anthropocene (p. 313). Similarly, Jesseka Batteau discusses "small acts of repair" in contemporary literature, where art attempts to heal or reconcile post-religious trauma (p. 319).

Early Traditions and Theoretical Perspectives
Ann Dooley analyzes early Irish lyrics, demonstrating how archaic poetic structures provide a framework for the "unravelling" and preservation of cultural memory over centuries (p. 327). The section concludes with Andreas Huyssen, who revisits the concept of "aesthetic autonomy" in the post-Adorno era, questioning how art maintains its critical distance and potential for remembrance in a contemporary world increasingly dominated by instrumental logic and mass mediation (p. 341).

Collectively, these chapters highlight that memory is not merely a cognitive process but a material and performed practice, where the specific form—whether a novel, a play, or a poem—fundamentally dictates what is remembered and how the past is mobilized.

The final section of the volume explores the intersection of memory and activism, moving from conceptual theories to the practical and political applications of remembrance. 

The Memory-Activism Nexus (pp. 349-392)

Social movements and activism: Priska Daphi observes how memory is mobilized by movements from below, while Stefan Berger examines the role of activist memory within the historical context of trade unionism. These contributions emphasize that memory is not a passive artifact but an active tool for social change.

Challenging forces: The collection investigates how civic memory is subject to pressure from state and market interests, with Samuel Merrill describing the "splintering" of this nexus. The impact of modern technology is also highlighted, as Thomas Smits analyzes how generative artificial intelligence alters the visual memory of protests.

Identity and material legacy: Anna (Amza) Reading addresses the importance of remembering and advocating for neurodivergent identities (autism) within the activist framework. Meanwhile, the physical and enduring nature of memory is discussed by Maria Grever, who posits that monuments function as "actants" that drive mnemonic change. 

The section concludes with Duygu Erbil and Clara Vlessing considering "Activist Afterlives," summarizing how the struggle to maintain and transmit legacies remains a continuous, evolving process beyond the initial moment of action. The work as a whole reaffirms Ann Rigney's influence, illustrating how cultural memory is constantly reframed, remediated, and mobilized to shape both present politics and future history.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the introduction, what is a defining characteristic of Ann Rigney's approach to studying cultural memory?
options: Viewing culture as a static archive of fixed, unchanging historical facts.
Conceptualizing memory as the outcome of ongoing, repeated acts of representation and mediation.
Focusing exclusively on individual psychological processes rather than external media.
Limiting analysis to 'official' history produced by professional historians.
questions.explanation: Rigney conceives of cultural memory as a dynamic, ongoing process rather than a static repository, emphasizing that it is 'in the works' and remediated over time.

questions.question: What does the text mean by an 'ecological approach' to memory studies?
options: Studying the impact of environmental climate change on the preservation of historical artifacts.
Focusing solely on the material decay of historical archives over time.
Analyzing the networks and interdependencies between various memory objects rather than studying them in isolation.
Prioritizing the study of natural landscapes as the primary site of cultural memory.
questions.explanation: An ecological approach, as referenced in the text, involves analyzing the broader networks and interactions within which memory objects operate and acquire meaning.

questions.question: In the context of the book, what is the significance of the term 'prememory' introduced by Guy Beiner?
options: The tendency of individuals to forget key historical events before they happen.
The study of how early Irish lyrics have been lost to modern readers.
The way specific acts of memory are prepared or performed in the present to shape future remembrance.
The psychological state of remembering an event that was never personally experienced.
questions.explanation: Beiner uses the concept of 'prememory' to describe how certain acts or preparations in the present lay the groundwork for how that past will be remembered in the future.

questions.question: Why does Ann Rigney use the term 'improper historian'?
options: To critique scholars who falsify historical records through intentional bias.
To challenge the idea that only professional historians are qualified to analyze history and its representation.
To describe historians who fail to use proper archival citations in their research.
To distinguish between academic historians and those who write historical fiction.
questions.explanation: Rigney uses this term to refute the misconception that 'history' is the sole domain of professional academic historians, broadening the field to include other forms of engagement with the past.

questions.question: What is the core argument behind the 'dynamics of memory' as discussed in the text?
options: Collective memory must constantly be negotiated and mediated to remain relevant in changing social contexts.
The most stable memories are those that are never recorded in media or art.
Cultural memory is purely dictated by government-sanctioned monuments and rituals.
The primary purpose of memory is to preserve the exact, original experience of a historical event.
questions.explanation: The 'dynamic turn' suggests memory is in constant flux, requiring continuous, active renegotiation to ensure it 'stays afloat' as it travels across different media and social contexts.

cards.front: What is the core definition of Ann Rigney's 'dynamics of memory'?
cards.back: Cultural memory is not a fixed repository, but an active, ongoing process of negotiation, remediation, and interpretation that must be constantly 'in the works' to remain relevant.

cards.front: How does Ann Rigney define the 'agency of the aesthetic'?
cards.back: The capacity of culture (literature, arts, media) to actively shape human experience, produce memory, and mobilize people.

cards.front: What does it mean to 'remediate' memory?
cards.back: The process of memory being filtered and transformed across multiple platforms, media technologies, and formats—traveling between words, images, and performances.
The study of how memory functions as a tool for political mobilization and how activist movements shape, challenge, or create memorial practices.

cards.front: What is an 'improper historian' according to Rigney's framework?
cards.back: A challenge to the idea that only professional historians are the legitimate authorities on the past, legitimizing other forms of memory-work and representation.

cards.front: What is 'prememory' in the context of memory studies?
cards.back: A concept identifying specific acts of memory (such as oratory or performance) that prepare the social and cultural ground for future recollection.

cards.front: How does an 'ecological approach' to memory function?
cards.back: It shifts focus from studying discrete or isolated memory objects to analyzing the interconnected networks, interdependencies, and relationships between material and symbolic elements.

cards.front: According to the introduction, what is the primary role of 'narrative' in historical memory?
cards.back: Narrative is a verbal structure and human construction that turns past events into coherent stories, acting as a filter for experience rather than a transparent window into history.

cards.front: What is the significance of the 'afterlives of memory'?
cards.back: The analysis of how themes and events continue to evolve, change meanings, and persist in cultural consciousness long after the initial event through ongoing retelling and creative engagement.

cards.front: How does the 'Platformization of memory' impact scholarly legacy?
cards.back: It refers to the digital transformation and mediation of memory through platforms, which significantly alters how memory is stored, accessed, and disseminated in the contemporary era.

slides.title: Dynamics, Mediation, and Mobilization
bullets: Memory as a dynamic, evolving cultural process (p. 14).
Mediation and remediation shape how we remember (p. 14).
Mobile memory links cultural forms to social mobilization (p. 14).
slides.notes: We explore how memory is not a fixed repository but an active, circulating process that shapes both culture and social change.

slides.title: The Dynamics of Memory
bullets: Collective memory is constantly 'in the works' (p. 17).
Processes of negotiation and (re)mediation define memory (p. 17).
Concepts like 'stickiness' explain why some narratives endure (p. 17-18).
Memory fluctuates based on socio-political and technological contexts (p. 17).
slides.notes: Memory functions like a swimmer—it must keep moving to stay afloat, defined by constant interpretation and differential memorability.

slides.title: Mediation and Remediation
bullets: Memory travels across diverse media and platforms (p. 14).
Cultural memory requires ongoing acts of transmission (p. 14).
Platforms transform scholarly legacy and historical access (p. 9).
Digital tools influence mnemonic 'stickiness' and circulation (p. 9).
slides.notes: Mediation is central to existence; memory is constantly remediated as it journeys through literature, platforms, and AI.

slides.title: The Memory-Activism Nexus
bullets: Memories drive social movements and activism from below (p. 13).
Protest slogans create and transmit cultural memory (p. 10).
Monuments serve as active agents for mnemonic change (p. 13).
Memory is a catalyst for political solidarity and justice (p. 10).
slides.notes: This section examines the symbiotic relationship between memory and active protest, demonstrating how the past is deployed to mobilize the present.
